paragraph: Rendex, a New York-based digital content rights management and licensing platform, raised a $16 million Series A led by Media Horizon Fund, with participation from Fulcrum Consumer Fund and several music industry executives participating as angel investors. Founded in 2021 by music rights attorney Simone Fabiano and software architect Kofi Asante, Rendex provides a SaaS platform that helps independent music publishers, film studios, and sports media rights holders catalog, license, and track commercial usage of their content catalogs programmatically. The platform integrates with global content identification systems, digital service provider reporting feeds, and sync licensing databases to give rights holders real-time visibility into where their content is being used, automate royalty calculations across multiple revenue streams, and generate digital licensing agreements via a templated workflow. Rendex said it manages rights catalogs containing 4.3 million works for 280 client organizations and that it has processed 1.1 million licensing transactions over the past 12 months. ARR stands at $4.8 million. Series A proceeds will fund the development of a blockchain-based rights ownership attestation module, expansion into the sports broadcast rights market, and the addition of 18 engineering and business development staff. The company currently employs 41 people.

paragraph: Brenta Systems, a Raleigh-based IT service management automation startup targeting the managed service provider market, closed a $24 million Series B led by Eastgate SaaS Growth Fund, with co-investment from Southeast Venture Partners and Rockford Capital. Founded in 2019 by MSP industry veterans Derek Thornton and Lisa Huang, Brenta provides a workflow automation and intelligent alert triage platform that sits above the PSA and RMM tools that MSPs use to manage client endpoints. The platform's AI triage engine ingests alerts from 12 integrated RMM platforms and uses historical resolution data from the MSP's own ticket history to automatically classify, prioritize, and route alerts to the appropriate tier of technical staff, suppressing noise that the engine predicts will self-resolve. Brenta said its platform reduces alert noise by an average of 71 percent and cuts mean-time-to-resolution for genuine incidents by 44 percent across its customer base. The company serves 195 MSPs managing a combined 1.8 million endpoints, and reported ARR of $7.6 million, up 220 percent year over year. Series B capital will fund the development of a client-facing reporting portal, a proactive maintenance automation module, and an international expansion starting with Australia and the United Kingdom. Brenta currently employs 68 people.

paragraph: Cadenza Pharma, a Lausanne-based drug discovery startup, announced a 56 million Swiss franc Series C led by BioAlps Investment Fund, with participation from Cavendish Life Sciences Fund and a major Japanese pharmaceutical company's venture unit. Founded by computational chemist Dr. Nadia Pellegrini and structural biologist Dr. Leon Kaufmann in 2017, Cadenza has built a structure-based drug design platform that combines cryo-electron microscopy derived protein structures with a proprietary molecular dynamics simulation engine to accelerate hit-to-lead optimization for difficult-to-drug protein targets. The company's lead oncology program, CAD-301, a selective degrader of a transcription factor implicated in aggressive forms of triple-negative breast cancer, has advanced to Phase I clinical trials at six sites in Switzerland, France, and Germany. Preliminary Phase I data showed tolerability across three dose cohorts with no dose-limiting toxicities and early evidence of target engagement at the two highest doses tested. The Series C proceeds will fund the completion of Phase I dose escalation, the initiation of a Phase Ib expansion cohort, and the advancement of Cadenza's second program, CAD-418, targeting a G protein-coupled receptor in pancreatic cancer. The company employs 92 scientists and clinical development specialists.

That safety and engagement profile from six Swiss, French, and German clinical sites positions the program well for Phase I completion and Phase Ib expansion cohort initiation. The computational platform that generated CAD-301, combining cryo-EM protein structures with a proprietary molecular dynamics simulation engine, is also advancing CAD-418, a GPCR-targeted program in pancreatic cancer. paragraph: Strobe Logistics, an Amsterdam-based autonomous last-mile delivery robotics company, raised 39 million euros in a Series B led by Eurotech Growth Partners, with co-investment from Portside Logistics Fund and the mobility investment arm of a major European postal operator. Founded in 2019 by roboticist Dr. Tariq Baroudi and autonomous systems engineer Elise Bergmann, Strobe has developed a six-wheeled sidewalk delivery robot — the Strobe Rover SR-3 — that navigates urban environments at speeds up to 6 kilometers per hour, carries a 15-kilogram payload, and operates for 10 hours on a single battery charge. The SR-3 uses a sensor suite combining lidar, stereo cameras, and ultrasonic sensors, and is powered by an onboard neural network trained on 280 million kilometers of simulated and real-world urban traversal data. Strobe has deployed 840 Rover units across Rotterdam, Brussels, Copenhagen, and Zurich under commercial partnerships with three major grocery chains and a pharmacy network. The company said Rovers completed 1.2 million deliveries in the prior 12 months with a 98.7 percent successful delivery rate. Series B proceeds will fund expansion of the fleet to 3,000 units, a rollout into London pending regulatory approval, and the development of a next-generation Rover platform with 40-kilogram payload capacity. Strobe employs 145 people.

paragraph: Vivara Home, a Los Angeles-based consumer proptech company, announced a $14 million Series A led by Westcoast Growth Capital, with participation from Consumer Frontier Fund and Hearthstone Ventures. Founded in 2022 by real estate data scientist Jade Morales and product designer Felix Okonkwo, Vivara provides a personalized home search and financing pre-qualification app that uses behavioral preference signals gathered during the browsing experience — including time-on-listing, saved amenity clusters, and commute-weight sliders — to build a latent preference model for each user and surface listings ranked by predicted match quality rather than recency or price. The platform integrates with 38 regional MLS data feeds covering 14 U.S. metropolitan areas, presents listings alongside a Vivara Match Score and an automated mortgage pre-qualification estimate powered by a soft-credit-pull API, and surfaces estimated total cost of ownership including property tax, HOA, and insurance projections. Vivara said it has 280,000 registered users, of whom 58,000 are active monthly, and that users who engage with the Match Score ranking spend 40 percent less time searching before submitting an offer. The Series A funds will be used to launch a buyer-agent referral marketplace and expand coverage to 22 additional metropolitan markets. Vivara employs 34 people.
restate_0: Vivara Home has raised $14 million in a Series A to scale its personalized home search platform beyond the 14 metropolitan markets it currently covers. The platform's Vivara Match Score sits alongside each listing together with an automated mortgage pre-qualification estimate and a total cost of ownership projection including taxes, HOA, and insurance. The commercial proof: users who engage with Match Score rankings spend 40 percent less time searching before submitting an offer. With 280,000 registered users and 58,000 monthly actives, a Series A from Westcoast Growth Capital with Consumer Frontier Fund and Hearthstone Ventures, and a buyer-agent referral marketplace on the product roadmap, Vivara is building toward a full home buying transaction platform.
restate_1: Felix and I founded Vivara in 2022 because we believed that the dominant home search paradigm — sort by price or recency, scroll indefinitely — was responsible for an enormous amount of unnecessary stress and wasted time for home buyers. The behavioral preference model behind Vivara is our answer: we watch how users interact with listings, cluster their saved amenities, interpret their commute weight settings, and build a latent preference model that gets better with every action. Our Match Score ranks listings by predicted match quality, and our users spend 40 percent less time searching before submitting an offer. With 280,000 registered users, 58,000 monthly actives, and integrations with 38 MLS data feeds across 14 metropolitan areas, we are announcing a $14 million Series A led by Westcoast Growth Capital. The buyer-agent referral marketplace is the commercial model expansion we have been designing for: connecting users who are ready to offer with agents who specialize in their target neighborhoods creates a transaction value that justifies significant investment. Coverage expansion to 22 additional metropolitan markets will compound the user growth that our behavioral model depends on. Our 34-person Los Angeles team is ready.
restate_2: INVESTMENT MEMO — Westcoast Growth Capital Series A Lead: Vivara Home. Recommendation: Lead at $14 million. Thesis: Consumer home search has remained largely unchanged in its core interface paradigm since the early days of Zillow-analogue platforms: price and recency sorting with static filters. Vivara's behavioral preference model — derived from time-on-listing, amenity clustering, and commute-weight signals — is a genuinely novel approach to surfacing listings that match revealed rather than stated preferences. The 40 percent search time reduction before offer submission is a behavioral efficiency metric that indicates real preference model accuracy and creates a clear consumer value proposition. At 280,000 registered users and 58,000 monthly actives, Vivara has early consumer scale with a user acquisition pattern that suggests word-of-mouth efficiency. The mortgage pre-qualification soft-pull API and total cost of ownership modeling materially increase the decision-making quality of the search experience and differentiate Vivara from pure listing discovery platforms. The buyer-agent referral marketplace is the monetization model: connecting ready buyers with local agent specialists generates commission referral revenue with high per-transaction value and aligns Vivara's success with completed transactions. Coverage expansion to 22 additional metropolitan markets is the growth investment. Key risk: Zillow-analogue platform replication of preference-based ranking. Mitigant: Vivara's behavioral data accumulation creates a compounding preference model advantage that late movers cannot quickly replicate.
restate_3: Vivara Home is making a smart bet against the standard home search paradigm. The 40 percent reduction in search time before offer submission is the product's commercial proof point and the metric that will drive referral and agent adoption. With 280,000 registered users, 58,000 monthly actives, and 38 MLS integrations across 14 metropolitan areas, Vivara has meaningful early scale for a 34-person Series A company. The $14 million from Westcoast Growth Capital, Consumer Frontier Fund, and Hearthstone Ventures funds the buyer-agent referral marketplace that converts the behavioral advantage into transaction revenue. The coverage expansion to 22 additional markets is the geographic investment that will compound the preference model's accuracy. paragraph: Avalis Networks, a Chicago-based telecommunications infrastructure startup, closed a $67 million Series D led by Midpoint Equity Partners and Greyrock Infrastructure Fund, with participation from Crestline Capital and a strategic commitment from a Tier 2 regional wireless carrier. Founded in 2016 by network engineer Brendan Callahan and RF systems architect Dr. Priya Suresh, Avalis has developed a software-defined radio access network platform — the Spectrion Fabric — that virtualizes 5G baseband and radio unit functions to run on commodity server hardware deployed in distributed edge locations, reducing the capital cost of deploying dense urban 5G networks by 55 percent compared to proprietary RAN equipment, according to the company's internal cost models. The Spectrion Fabric is currently deployed across 4,800 radio sites serving two carrier customers in the United States and one in the United Kingdom, supporting 3.1 million end users. Avalis reported ARR of $42 million, up 98 percent from the prior fiscal year, and said it has a contracted backlog of $180 million in software and services revenue. The Series D proceeds will fund a buildout of the company's systems integration team to 150 professionals, the porting of the Spectrion Fabric to additional processor architectures including ARM-based server silicon, and the pursuit of network equipment certification in Japan and South Korea. Avalis employs 320 people across Chicago, London, and Tokyo.

Led by Midpoint Equity Partners and Greyrock Infrastructure Fund with Crestline Capital and a Tier 2 carrier participating strategically, the Series D funds a 150-person systems integration team, ARM-based server silicon porting, and Japan and South Korea network certification. The Tokyo office reflects the strategic priority of the Asian market.

The $67 million Series D co-led by Midpoint Equity Partners and Greyrock Infrastructure Fund gives us the capital to build our systems integration team to 150 professionals, port Spectrion Fabric to ARM-based server silicon, and pursue certification in Japan and South Korea. Our 320 people across Chicago, London, and Tokyo are building the open 5G infrastructure that the global telecom industry needs.
restate_2: INVESTMENT MEMO — Midpoint Equity Partners and Greyrock Infrastructure Fund Series D Co-Lead: Avalis Networks. Recommendation: Co-lead at $67 million. Thesis: Open RAN and virtual RAN represent the largest structural shift in telecommunications infrastructure economics in a generation, with the potential to reduce 5G network capital expenditure by 30 to 60 percent through software-defined, commodity-hardware-based RAN deployments. Avalis's Spectrion Fabric is among the most commercially deployed vRAN platforms globally, with 4,800 sites, three carrier customers, and 3.1 million end users providing real-world scale validation at commercial quality. Japan and South Korea represent the highest-technology wireless markets globally and are the largest near-term whitespace for Spectrion Fabric deployment among Tier 1 carriers. Tier 2 carrier strategic participation provides deployment data from the specific customer segment with the most acute need for CapEx reduction.
